programs.name: ARKansas Medicaid
programs.what_it_does: Provides medical coverage for eligible seniors and disabled individuals, including long-term services and supports. It helps pay for doctor visits, hospital stays, prescription drugs, and nursing home care.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors aged 65+ and disabled adults meeting income and asset limits.
programs.benefit_value: Varies by eligibility; covers most Medicaid services at little to no cost.

programs.name: ARChoices in Homecare
programs.what_it_does: Offers home and community-based services waiver program as an alternative to nursing homes. Provides personal care, adult day care, respite, and homemaker services to support independent living.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Medicaid-eligible seniors 65+ or disabled adults needing nursing home level of care but preferring to stay at home.
programs.benefit_value: Up to 40-60 hours/week of services based on assessed needs; no cost for eligible.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.what_it_does: Delivers comprehensive medical and social services to frail seniors in community settings. Includes adult day health, transportation, home care, and hospital coordination to avoid institutionalization.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 55+ certified as nursing facility eligible, able to live safely in community with supports.
programs.benefit_value: All-inclusive capitated coverage; full Medicare/Medicaid benefits for enrollees.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.what_it_does: Helps pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copayments for low-income beneficiaries. QMB covers Part A/B premiums and costs; SLMB/QI cover Part B premiums.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Seniors 65+ with Medicare meeting specific income/asset limits (e.g., QMB up to 100% FPL).
programs.benefit_value: $174.70/month Part B premium savings (2026); full cost-sharing for QMB.

programs.name: SNAP (Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Provides monthly benefits via EBT card to buy healthy food at approved stores. Administered statewide through DHS for eligible low-income households.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ or disabled with income below 130-200% FPL depending on household.
programs.benefit_value: Average $200-300/month per senior household; max up to $291/single (2026 adjusted).

programs.name: LIHEAP (Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program)
programs.what_it_does: Assists with heating/cooling bills, crisis intervention, and weatherproofing for low-income homes. Funds payments directly to utility providers.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income households including seniors at or below 150% FPL with high energy costs.
programs.benefit_value: $200-1,000 annual benefit per household based on fuel type and income.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program (WAP)
programs.what_it_does: Improves energy efficiency of low-income homes through insulation, sealing, and furnace repairs. Reduces utility bills and health risks from poor conditions.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors and families at or below 200% FPL, prioritizing elderly/disabled.
programs.benefit_value: Free services worth $5,000-10,000 per home in energy upgrades.

programs.name: Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP) / SHIP
programs.what_it_does: Offers free Medicare counseling, fraud prevention training, and enrollment assistance. Helps seniors understand options and avoid scams.[1]
programs.who_its_for: All Medicare beneficiaries 65+, especially those needing plan comparisons or billing help.
programs.benefit_value: Free one-on-one counseling; potential savings of thousands via proper enrollment.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.what_it_does: Delivers nutritious meals to homebound seniors through Area Agencies on Aging. Provides 1-2 hot meals daily plus social wellness checks.[4][5]
programs.who_its_for: Homebound seniors 60+ unable to prepare meals, often low-income prioritized.
programs.benefit_value: 5-10 meals/week at no/low cost (often free for low-income); $5-7/meal value.

programs.name: Family Caregiver Support Program
programs.what_it_does: Provides respite care, training, and counseling to relieve family caregivers. Funds temporary relief to prevent caregiver burnout.[4]
programs.who_its_for: Unpaid family caregivers of seniors 60+ or disabled adults at risk of nursing home placement.
programs.benefit_value: Respite up to $30-40/hour or vouchers; training at no cost.

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)
programs.what_it_does: Offers part-time community service jobs with training for unemployed low-income seniors. Helps build skills for unsubsidized employment.[1][3]
programs.who_its_for: Unemployed low-income seniors 55+ with barriers to employment.
programs.benefit_value: Minimum wage pay (20 hrs/week) plus benefits; avg $7,000/year income.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors (Area Agency Programs)
programs.what_it_does: Provides free legal advice, representation for benefits, housing, and abuse issues. Offered through local AAAs and Legal Aid of Arkansas.[5]
programs.who_its_for: Low-income seniors 60+ facing civil legal problems.
programs.benefit_value: Free services worth thousands in attorney fees.

programs.name: Long-term Care Ombudsman
programs.what_it_does: Advocates for residents in nursing homes and assisted living resolving complaints. Protects rights and ensures quality care.[1]
programs.who_its_for: Residents/family in AR long-term care facilities; all seniors welcome to contact.
programs.benefit_value: Free advocacy resolving 80%+ complaints without litigation.
